summary: Machine policy for lock-screen camera access. The local Microsoft ControlPanelDisplay.admx file documents the same Personalization policy key and NoLockScreenCamera value used by the app.

setting.name: Lock Screen Camera Access
setting.casual_explanation: Windows can allow a camera to be used directly from the lock screen. This policy decides whether that lock-screen camera access can be enabled.
setting.why_people_change_it: People disable it to keep the lock screen simpler, reduce camera exposure before sign-in, or match stricter privacy policies.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Turning it off reduces pre-sign-in camera access, but it also removes a convenience feature for users who want to use the camera from the lock screen.
setting.professional_notes: Microsoft documents NoLockScreenCamera through ADMX_ControlPanelDisplay and local policy files map it to HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Personalization\NoLockScreenCamera. The public documentation clearly explains the enabled policy behavior and the not-configured or disabled baseline behavior, but does not publish a raw DWORD table, so this record models the documented enforced state plus the default unset state.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: Users can still enable lock-screen camera access because the device is not forcing this policy.

allowed_values.label: Prevent lock-screen camera access
allowed_values.meaning: The policy is enabled and users cannot enable or invoke camera access from the lock screen.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes NoLockScreenCamera = 1, which matches the documented enabled policy state.

windows_defaults.label: Windows machine-policy default
windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows 10 version 2004 with KB5005101 and later, plus Windows 11 systems exposing ADMX_ControlPanelDisplay

states.rationale: The public Microsoft policy documentation describes camera access as available when the policy is disabled or not configured.

windows_defaults.notes: The default behavior does not force lock-screen camera access off.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
intended_for: Users who want lock-screen camera access available

states.rationale: Leave the lock-screen camera policy unset.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want the normal Windows lock-screen camera option to remain available.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You do not want camera access exposed before sign-in.
tradeoffs: Users can still choose to enable lock-screen camera access.
There is no policy-based restriction on the feature.

recommended_profiles.label: No pre-sign-in camera
avoid_for: Users who rely on invoking the camera from the lock screen

states.rationale: Prevent lock-screen camera access through policy.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want camera access blocked before the user signs in.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want the convenience of launching the camera from the lock screen.
tradeoffs: Users lose the lock-screen camera convenience feature.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft ControlPanelDisplay.admx mapping
evidence.summary: The local ADMX file maps the machine policy to HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Personalization\NoLockScreenCamera.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft ControlPanelDisplay.adml help text
evidence.summary: The local ADML help text says enabling the policy disables the lock-screen camera toggle and prevents a camera from being invoked on the lock screen, while users can enable it by default.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes NoLockScreenCamera = 1 for the current tweak.

evidence.title: Existing tweak source record
evidence.summary: The repo already tracks provenance for this tweak in the current catalog output.

decision.why: The local Microsoft ControlPanelDisplay.admx file explicitly defines NoLockScreenCamera under Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Personalization, and the paired ADML help text documents that enabling the policy blocks lock-screen camera access. The app writes that exact enabled state.

validation_proof.notes: The local official Microsoft ADMX file names the same machine policy surface used by the app. The paired ADML help text states that enabling the setting prevents a camera from being invoked on the lock screen.
